changelog: v1.2 (2026-07-09): (1) split 'teaching_enjoyment' into itself (renamed 'Teaching / Explaining Enjoyment') plus new 'public_visibility_comfort', after a real user's private-teaching-only answers registered as a full match for Developer Relations' public-visibility-defining trait. (2) Added 4 new skill dimensions — ml_engineering_fluency, mobile_platform_fluency, data_infrastructure_fluency, cloud_infrastructure_fluency — after the same user's ML Engineer #1 match had zero relationship to their self-reported (non-ML) domain background; these mirror the existing physical_constraint_engineering/adversarial_threat_modeling pattern of a real domain-fluency gate rather than a personality trait. v1.1 (2026-07-09): split 'account_relationship_ownership' into 'account_portfolio_breadth' and 'relationship_continuity' after real-user bug report + JD-cache research (149 real 2026 postings) showed the single axis conflated two independent patterns (e.g. Forward Deployed Engineer and Customer Success Engineer both scored target=5 despite CSE being wide-portfolio and FDE being narrow-embed). See docs/research/dimensions-v1.1-relationship-split.md.

dimensions.name: Ambiguity Tolerance
dimensions.definition: Comfort operating without a pre-defined spec, backlog, or crisp success metric — defining the problem, scope, and even what 'success' means as part of the job, versus wanting well-scoped work with clear, agreed acceptance criteria handed down. Includes tolerance for contested or qualitative success metrics, not just undefined technical scope.

anchors.1: Wants a ticket with clear acceptance criteria (or an audited numeric target like an SLA) before starting; ambiguous asks or contested success metrics feel uncomfortable or blocking.
anchors.3: Can work from a rough one-paragraph brief or a portfolio of multiple KPIs and fill in details, but wants the underlying goal or a defined deliverable to crystallize before/while executing.
anchors.5: Thrives being handed a vague business outcome or 'figure out the problem yourself' mandate with no defined technical approach and no single agreed metric; enjoys defining the problem, and the criteria for solving it, as much as solving it.

dimensions.name: Interrupt Tolerance
dimensions.definition: How much a person's ideal workday tolerates being pulled out of focused work by external demands arriving on someone else's timeline (Slack, tickets, stakeholder requests, in-progress incidents) versus needing long, self-scheduled, uninterrupted blocks. This is about daytime attention fragmentation and work cadence generally, distinct from the specific off-hours paging captured in On-Call/Incident Appetite.

anchors.1: Wants a fully self-scheduled calendar; can block off days or weeks for planned work with no expectation of same-day interruption, and resents unplanned context switches.
anchors.3: Comfortable with scheduled, plannable interruptions (standups, PR review, workshops, SOW milestones) and will drop them occasionally for real urgency, but expects most of the week to be self-directed.
anchors.5: Work arrives primarily via an inbound queue, page, or live urgent request with a clock running; is energized by fielding a stream of unplanned demands all day rather than experiencing them as a tax on 'real work.'

dimensions.name: On-Call / Incident Appetite
dimensions.definition: Willingness and psychological comfort with being paged for production or field issues — including nights/weekends — and doing live incident response under real business, safety, or security pressure. Distinct from Interrupt Tolerance: a person can enjoy the adrenaline of an on-call page while disliking routine daytime interruptions, or vice versa.

anchors.1: Actively avoids any on-call responsibility; a job requiring nights/weekends paging or crisis response is a dealbreaker.
anchors.3: Will do on-call as part of a fair rotation with reasonable tooling/escalation support for moderate-urgency issues (e.g., crash-rate spikes), but doesn't seek out high-stakes incidents.
anchors.5: Comfortable owning live, high-stakes incidents in real time — production outages, security breaches, or safety-critical field failures with organizational or legal visibility — and may find the pressure and problem-solving rewarding rather than distressing.

dimensions.name: Debugging / Diagnostic Depth
dimensions.definition: Skill at diagnosing unfamiliar technical problems quickly using production-grade tooling (logs, traces, SQL, hardware debuggers, feature flags), especially when the root cause could span multiple ambiguous layers (application vs. infrastructure, hardware vs. firmware, code vs. adversary) before a fix is even possible.

anchors.1: Root cause is nearly always legible from application-level logs or stack traces; debugging is single-layer and rarely time-pressured.
anchors.3: Regularly debugs real technical problems that cross a layer or two (app code and third-party/OS behavior, or a workshop-surfaced integration issue) with room to research before resolving.
anchors.5: Routinely diagnoses and fixes unfamiliar production issues within minutes to hours under an SLA or live-audience clock, forming and discarding hypotheses across fundamentally different failure domains (hardware, firmware, network, adversarial intent) as needed.

dimensions.name: Systems Design at Scale
dimensions.definition: Skill at designing structures (services, data models, pipelines, platforms) that hold up as scale, load, or organizational complexity grows, as opposed to tactical, feature-level implementation skill.

anchors.1: Comfortable implementing well-specified components; hasn't designed a system that needed to survive significant scale or multi-team dependency.
anchors.3: Can design a service, pipeline, or schema that works well for a single team's current scale; needs support extrapolating far beyond current load.
anchors.5: Regularly designs systems or platforms intended to serve many consuming teams or scale multiple orders of magnitude, and is judged on whether that design held up over time.

dimensions.name: Technical Breadth vs. Depth
dimensions.definition: Preference and skill for going deep in one technology, platform, or discipline (a single distributed system, one vendor's product, one ML framework, or a second formal discipline like electrical engineering) versus ranging fluidly across many layers, platforms, or vendors as the situation demands.

anchors.1: Deep specialist in one narrow technical layer, platform, or discipline; prefers to keep building expertise there rather than context-switch, even if it requires formal study of a second discipline (e.g., EE/RF for embedded work).
anchors.3: Comfortable in 2-3 adjacent layers or platforms (e.g., backend + data, or deep on one vendor's stack applied across many customer domains).
anchors.5: Moves fluidly across many platforms, vendors, clouds, or stack layers as needed; prefers variety over specialization and readily picks up whatever a given engagement requires.

dimensions.name: Outcome Accountability
dimensions.definition: Whether a person's sense of responsibility is anchored to a measurable external outcome they can be held accountable for in production (a KPI, an SLO, a deployed system's uptime, a business result) even when they don't fully control every contributing factor, versus stewardship of a system's technical soundness or a design/recommendation whose real-world outcome is someone else's to own. Includes where in the customer lifecycle that accountability sits (pre-contract vs. post-sale/delivery) and whether motivation centers on the system's craft versus the business/user outcome it enables.

anchors.1: Accountability ends at a technical win, a design document, or a handed-off recommendation; motivated by the system's technical soundness for its own sake, and uncomfortable being on the hook for a metric influenced by factors outside their control.
anchors.3: Comfortable owning a metric, SLA, or project deliverable for a system they mostly control, and may span from late pre-sale into early implementation, but wants clear attribution before accepting blame for what happens after handoff.
anchors.5: Readily accepts being the named owner of a KPI/SLO/production incident from post-sale through sustained operation, even when upstream factors they don't control contribute; treats technology as a means to a business/user end and that accountability as motivating rather than unfair.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort
dimensions.definition: Comfort and skill operating as a service provider to others — whether internal engineering teams treated as customers, or external clients ranging from skeptical practitioners to business executives — including adapting communication register across that range as a sustained daily mode rather than an occasional task.

anchors.1: Prefers to be handed requirements and left alone to build; uncomfortable 'selling' adoption of their own work or fielding open-ended requests from other teams, and executive- or client-facing communication is rare.
anchors.3: Willing to gather requirements and communicate with other teams or a client's technical practitioners when the process is structured (tickets, planning cycles, workshops), but sustained day-to-day counterparts are technical peers, not executives.
anchors.5: Actively enjoys the requirements-gathering/advocacy/adoption loop of treating other engineers or clients as customers, and fluidly shifts register across a skeptical engineer, a procurement stakeholder, and a C-suite sponsor, often within the same week.

dimensions.name: Teaching / Explaining Enjoyment
dimensions.definition: Enjoyment of and skill at translating technical concepts into terms a non-expert or unfamiliar audience can act on, as a primary job output rather than an incidental byproduct of having the knowledge. Deliberately silent on audience size or publicness — a person can score high here while only ever teaching one customer's team at a time (see Public Visibility Comfort for that separate axis).

anchors.1: Communicates mainly with technical peers; no explicit expectation of translating concepts for non-experts.
anchors.3: Regularly explains technical concepts to one customer or account's team as part of delivery (workshops, support replies, enablement plans), and finds this a genuine, not merely tolerated, part of the job.
anchors.5: Primary job output is teaching or enablement content — tutorials, workshops, onboarding material — designed for an audience who wasn't in the room, and this is a named, evaluated, and enjoyed part of the job, regardless of whether that audience is one account or the public.

dimensions.name: Public Visibility Comfort
dimensions.definition: Comfort being a visible, named, one-to-many public presence — conference talks, published content under one's own name, open community engagement (social media, forums, Discord) — as opposed to work that stays private between the practitioner and a specific customer, account, or team, however much teaching or explaining that private work involves. This was split out from Teaching / Explaining Enjoyment (v1.2) after real-user testing showed the merged dimension let private-teaching-only answers register as a full match for public-facing roles like Developer Relations.

anchors.1: All interactions are private, 1:1, or 1:few with a specific customer, account, or team; no public content, social presence, or speaking expectation, and this person has no interest in building one.
anchors.3: Occasional internal cross-team presentation, or a single conference talk/post as a rare event, not a sustained public presence.
anchors.5: Regularly public and visible under their own name — conference talks, published content, active social/community presence — and actively wants this, tolerating the exposure and scrutiny that comes with it as a real part of the job.

dimensions.name: Account Portfolio Breadth
dimensions.definition: How many concurrent client/account relationships a person is juggling at once, from one deep engagement at a time to a large, fast-rotating portfolio, territory, or book of many simultaneous relationships. Independent of how long any single relationship lasts (see Relationship Continuity) — a role can be narrow-and-brief, narrow-and-indefinite, wide-and-brief, or wide-and-indefinite.

anchors.1: One relationship or engagement at a time; full attention on a single account before (if ever) moving to the next.
anchors.3: A handful of concurrent named accounts, or a moderate caseload of simultaneous engagements, being actively managed at once.
anchors.5: A large, fast-rotating portfolio, territory, or book of many concurrent relationships — the organizing unit of the job is the whole book, not any single account.

dimensions.name: Relationship Continuity
dimensions.definition: Whether the unit of work is an anonymous, one-off interaction or a named relationship with real duration — and if so, whether that duration is bounded to a defined project/engagement or open-ended and indefinite. Independent of how many such relationships exist at once (see Account Portfolio Breadth).

anchors.1: Each interaction is with an effectively anonymous, non-repeating counterpart; no expectation of relationship history or follow-up.
anchors.3: A real, named relationship exists but is bounded to a defined engagement with a planned end (a project, an SOW, an onboarding sprint, a sales cycle) — ownership is explicitly handed off to someone else once it concludes.
anchors.5: An open-ended, indefinite relationship with the same named account(s), building trust and continuity over time with no planned end date.

dimensions.name: Variable Compensation Appetite
dimensions.definition: Comfort with, and preference for, compensation that is directly and periodically tied to an external, partly-out-of-one's-control business outcome — OTE-style quota/commission attainment, or (in a consulting context) a tracked billable-utilization target — rather than a stable salary with at most a modest, outcome-decoupled bonus.

anchors.1: Wants compensation fully decoupled from any quota/commission outcome or hour-tracked utilization target; would be unsettled by a bonus tied to deal-closing, OTE attainment, or billable-hours performance.
anchors.3: Comfortable with a modest bonus tied to a proxy like utilization or margin, or occasional variable comp, but not OTE-style quota/commission risk.
anchors.5: Actively wants meaningful upside/downside tied to OTE-style quota/commission attainment (or, in a consulting context, a tracked billable-utilization target); treats variable comp as a feature, not a risk.

dimensions.name: Coding Intensity
dimensions.definition: The share of a typical week spent personally writing, reviewing, or debugging production (or production-bound) code, and how much residual hands-on-keyboard fluency the role requires versus reading/reasoning about others' code or letting that fluency fade.

anchors.1: Little to no hands-on coding; role is almost entirely advisory, design, or people-management work, and hands-on fluency is not expected to be maintained.
anchors.3: Codes to prototype, demo, or validate technical fit, or reads code/specs closely enough to judge estimates and architecture, but this is not the dominant weekly activity and isn't what ships.
anchors.5: Coding is the dominant weekly activity and the code produced is the actual production deliverable a customer or the business runs on.

dimensions.name: Travel / Physical Embed Willingness
dimensions.definition: Willingness to spend a substantial share of working time traveling to, or physically embedded at, a client or customer site rather than working from a home base.

anchors.1: Wants to work from one location; travel is a hard constraint or dealbreaker.
anchors.3: Comfortable with periodic travel for key moments (finalist presentations, kickoffs, a conference) but expects a mostly home-based rhythm most weeks.
anchors.5: Comfortable being on the road or physically embedded at a client site the majority of working weeks, potentially for extended stretches.

dimensions.name: Adversarial / Threat-Model Thinking
dimensions.definition: Default orientation toward reasoning about what a motivated adversary would do to break or exploit a system, as opposed to reasoning primarily about the most likely accidental failure mode.

anchors.1: Never models adversarial behavior; all reasoning is about accidental or random failure modes.
anchors.3: Occasionally considers abuse cases (input validation, basic auth) as part of a broader engineering job, not a primary lens.
anchors.5: Default mental model for any system is 'how would someone try to break or exploit this'; runs threat modeling and risk assessments as a primary work product.

dimensions.name: Physical Constraint Engineering
dimensions.definition: Comfort and skill reasoning about hard physical resource budgets (power, memory, real-time deadlines, RF/signal behavior) as pass/fail constraints rather than soft optimization targets, including debugging at the hardware/software boundary.

anchors.1: Physical constraints are irrelevant to daily work; optimizes purely for logical/software correctness.
anchors.3: Aware of soft resource budgets (app size, battery drain, cold-start time) tracked as CI-gated metrics, but not safety-critical hard-stops.
anchors.5: Treats power/memory/timing budgets as absolute pass/fail gates; routinely debugs at the hardware/software boundary with oscilloscopes, logic analyzers, or JTAG.

dimensions.name: ML Engineering Fluency
dimensions.definition: Self-rated hands-on experience building, training, deploying, or productionizing machine learning systems — data pipelines feeding models, model training/evaluation, or ML serving infrastructure — as opposed to theoretical familiarity or none at all. Added in v1.2 after real-user testing showed ML Engineer could rank #1 purely on generic senior-engineer temperament (ambiguity tolerance, systems design, coding intensity) with zero check against actual ML background, since no prior dimension measured this directly. Mirrors the existing pattern of Physical Constraint Engineering (Embedded) and Adversarial Threat Modeling (Security) — a real domain-fluency gate, not a personality trait.

anchors.1: No hands-on experience with ML or data-pipeline work; machine learning has not been part of what I've built.
anchors.3: Some working exposure — used ML APIs/libraries or built a model or pipeline component, but not as the primary focus of a role.
anchors.5: Deep, current hands-on experience — I've built, trained, deployed, or maintained production ML systems as a core, ongoing part of my work.

dimensions.name: Mobile Platform Fluency
dimensions.definition: Self-rated hands-on experience building native or cross-platform mobile applications (iOS/Android), as opposed to web/backend/other platforms. Added in v1.2 alongside ML Engineering Fluency for the same reason: Mobile Engineer had no dimension directly measuring actual mobile-platform background.

anchors.1: No hands-on mobile app development experience.
anchors.3: Some exposure — built a small app or contributed to a mobile codebase, but not a primary focus of a role.
anchors.5: Deep, current hands-on experience — I've built and shipped mobile apps as a core, ongoing part of my work.

dimensions.name: Data Infrastructure Fluency
dimensions.definition: Self-rated hands-on experience building and operating data pipelines, warehouses, or ETL/ELT infrastructure, as opposed to just consuming data or none at all. Added in v1.2 for the same reason as ML Engineering Fluency and Mobile Platform Fluency — Data Engineer previously had no dimension directly measuring actual data-infrastructure background.

anchors.1: No hands-on experience building data pipelines or warehouse infrastructure.
anchors.3: Some exposure — written ETL/ELT jobs or queried/maintained a warehouse, but not as a primary focus of a role.
anchors.5: Deep, current hands-on experience — I've built and operated production data pipelines or warehouse infrastructure as a core, ongoing part of my work.

dimensions.name: Cloud Infrastructure Fluency
dimensions.definition: Self-rated hands-on experience building and operating cloud infrastructure, internal developer platforms, or CI/CD systems, as opposed to only using infrastructure someone else built. Added in v1.2 for the same reason as the other domain-fluency dimensions — Platform/Infrastructure Engineer previously had no dimension directly measuring actual infra-building background (Systems Design at Scale measures a related but distinct architectural-thinking skill, not hands-on tool experience).

anchors.1: No hands-on experience building or operating cloud/infrastructure systems.
anchors.3: Some exposure — configured cloud resources or CI/CD for my own team's use, but not as a primary focus of a role.
anchors.5: Deep, current hands-on experience — I've built and operated cloud infrastructure or internal developer platforms as a core, ongoing part of my work.

dimensions.name: People-Outcome Management Orientation
dimensions.definition: The degree to which a person wants their success to be defined by the growth, performance, and retention of other people rather than by their own individual technical output, including willingness to absorb the emotional and organizational labor (conflict, stress, politics) that comes with that responsibility.

anchors.1: Wants success judged almost entirely on personal output; managing others' growth, morale, or conflicts holds no appeal and would feel like a distraction from real work.
anchors.3: Enjoys informally mentoring or leading a workstream and can handle occasional team friction, but wants their own evaluation to stay primarily about personal deliverables.
anchors.5: Wants a formal role where team retention, morale, growth, and hiring are the actual basis of their own performance review, and is willing to make absorbing team stress and navigating conflict a core, daily part of the job.
